title: Recursion
description: Master recursive thinking with base cases, memoization using HashMap, backtracking patterns, and converting recursion to iteration.

sections.title: Base Case and Recursive Case
sections.explanation: Every recursive function needs: (1) a base case that returns without recursion, (2) a recursive case that moves toward the base case. Without a proper base case, you get infinite recursion and StackOverflowError. Think of recursion as delegating the smaller subproblem to a recursive call and combining the results. Classic examples: factorial, fibonacci, sum of array.

exercise.prompt: Write a recursive static method `power(int base, int exp)` that computes base^exp. Base case: exp == 0 returns 1. Recursive case: base * power(base, exp-1). Test with power(2, 10) and power(3, 4).

validation.message: Include a base case for exp==0 and a recursive call with exp-1.

exercise.hint: if (exp == 0) return 1; else return base * power(base, exp - 1);

sections.title: HashMap Memoization
sections.explanation: Memoization caches previously computed results to avoid redundant work. Use `HashMap<Input, Result>` passed to the recursive function or as a class field. Before computing, check if the answer is in the memo. After computing, store it. Transforms O(2^n) fibonacci into O(n). Essential for dynamic programming problems in interviews.

exercise.prompt: Write a memoized recursive method `climbStairs(int n, HashMap<Integer, Integer> memo)` that returns the number of ways to climb n stairs taking 1 or 2 steps at a time. Base cases: n==0 returns 1, n<0 returns 0. Test with n=5 and n=10.

validation.message: Check memo before computing, store result in memo, recurse with n-1 and n-2.

exercise.hint: if (n==0) return 1; if (n<0) return 0; Check memo, then result = climbStairs(n-1) + climbStairs(n-2). Store in memo.

sections.title: Backtracking
sections.explanation: Backtracking builds solutions incrementally and abandons paths that can't lead to a valid solution. Pattern: (1) check if current state is a complete solution, (2) try each option, (3) recurse with the option added, (4) undo the option (backtrack). Used for permutations, subsets, N-queens, Sudoku. Key: always undo your choice after the recursive call.

exercise.prompt: Write a backtracking method that generates all permutations of the string "abc". Use a boolean[] used array to track which characters are in the current permutation. Print each permutation on its own line. There should be 6 permutations.

validation.expected:
abc
acb
bac
bca
cab
cba
validation.message: All 6 permutations of abc in lexicographic order.
Mark used, append, recurse, then undo both (deleteCharAt and used=false).

exercise.hint: Base: current.length()==s.length() -> add to result. Loop i 0..2: if !used[i], set used[i]=true, append s.charAt(i), recurse, deleteCharAt, set used[i]=false.

sections.title: Converting Recursion to Stack-Based Iteration
sections.explanation: Any recursion can be converted to iteration using an explicit stack. This avoids StackOverflowError for deep recursion. Push the initial state onto the stack. While stack is not empty: pop, process, push sub-problems. Order of pushing matters (reverse order to simulate call order). Common interview follow-up: "can you do it iteratively?"

exercise.prompt: Convert the recursive sum-of-digits function to iterative using a stack. For a number like 1234, push each digit onto a stack then pop and sum them all. Test with 1234 (sum=10) and 9876 (sum=30).

validation.message: Use push/pop with a stack and % 10 to extract digits.

exercise.hint: While n > 0: push(n % 10), n /= 10. Then while !isEmpty: sum += pop().
